narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 32, along with your lower-middle family income background and plans to attend full-time, you've put real effort into your law school application. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicted an 88.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key benchmark used to evaluate applications like yours. This high risk of failure led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass the bar and succeed in their legal careers. The model's decision was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school—specifically, your Year 3 decile of 1, which had the strongest negative pull by signaling lower performance relative to peers and pushing the prediction heavily toward failure. Your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, further indicating that your early grades placed you in the bottom tier compared to the typical student's mid-range standing around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score came in third in influence, and since it's below the average of about 37, it added to the model's worries about your readiness for the rigors ahead. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA slightly above many applicants nudged the prediction toward success, while your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%) and offered a small positive boost. Your family income level had minimal sway but didn't help counter the academic red flags. Compared to typical students who get approved, your deciles are notably lower, placing you well below the averages and in a riskier category for bar passage. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your determination—many factors like these early grades can improve with targeted effort. To move forward, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to strengthen your performance record, and perhaps exploring part-time options or prep courses that have helped others in similar situations turn things around. You've got this—law school doors can reopen with the right steps. Keep pushing.
